items.title: Scoliosis classification: idiopathic vs congenital
items.contentHtml:
Classification of scoliosis (idiopathic vs congenital)

  • Idiopathic scoliosis: no identifiable cause; subclassification by age: infantile (0-3 years), juvenile (4-9 years) and adolescent (10-18 years).
  • Congenital scoliosis: secondary to embryological vertebral anomalies such as formation defects (hemivertebra) or segmentation defects (unilateral bar).
  • Key differential diagnosis: congenital scoliosis usually progresses more rapidly and requires early evaluation of the urinary tract (association with renal malformations in 20-30%).
  • Examination trap: idiopathic scoliosis does not present neurological alterations at the beginning; If there is a deficit, rule out syringomyelia or tumor.

items.title: Cobb angle: measurement and thresholds
items.contentHtml:
Cobb angle in scoliosis

  • Cobb angle: standard method to quantify the magnitude of the scoliotic curve in anteroposterior radiography of the total spine.
  • It is measured by drawing a line perpendicular to the upper limit vertebra (more inclined) and another perpendicular to the inferior limit vertebra; the angle of intersection is the Cobb angle.
  • Threshold of significant progression: increase >5 degrees in two consecutive measurements.
  • Indication for orthopedic treatment: Cobb angle >20-25 degrees in skeletally immature patient (Risser 0-2).
  • Surgical indication: Cobb angle >45-50 degrees in Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis.

items.title: Adams test: structural vs functional scoliosis
items.contentHtml:
Adams test and structural scoliosis

  • Adams test (anterior flexion test): patient flexes the trunk forward with extended knees; asymmetry of the thorax or rib gibbosity is observed.
  • If gibbosity (unilateral prominence) appears, the scoliosis is structural (fixed curve with vertebral rotation).
  • If there is no gibbosity (curve disappears in flexion), it is functional scoliosis (postural, due to lower limb length discrepancy).
  • Trap: the Adams test is screening; does not quantify the Cobb angle; a positive gibbosity requires an x-ray.
  • Structural scoliosis has vertebral rotation that persists in recumbency, while functional scoliosis is corrected when the patient sits up.

items.title: Major curve and compensating curve
items.contentHtml:
Major curve and compensatory curve

  • Major curve: the one of greatest magnitude (largest Cobb angle) and structural (fixed, not completely corrected in lateral inclination).
  • Compensatory curve: secondary curve that maintains the global balance of the trunk; It is usually flexible (it is corrected by >50% in bending).
  • In the pre-surgical assessment, the compensatory curve can be included in the fusion if it is >25 degrees and rigid.
  • Examination trap: the compensatory curve not should be confused with the greater curve; Fusion of the compensatory curve unnecessarily limits lumbar mobility.
  • Lenke's classification uses the relationship between major and minor curves.

items.title: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS)
items.contentHtml:
Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (AIS)

  • Definition: idiopathic scoliosis diagnosed between 10 and 18 years; It is the most common type (80-85% of all idiopathic scoliosis).
  • Epidemiology: more common in women (ratio 4:1 for curves >20 degrees).
  • Lenke classification: based on curve type (1-6), lumbar modifier (A, B, C) and sagittal thoracic (-, N, +).
  • Progression: higher risk in curves >25 degrees and Risser 0-2; the growth spurt accelerates progression.
  • Treatment: observation (<20 degrees), corset (>20-25 degrees in immature), surgery (>45-50 degrees).

items.title: Surgical indications in scoliosis
items.contentHtml:
Surgical indications in scoliosis

  • Greater curve with Cobb angle >45-50 degrees in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (risk of progression in adulthood).
  • Documented progression >5 degrees/year in skeletally immature patient despite corset treatment.
  • Intractable pain or significant truncal decompensation (C7 plumb deviated >2 cm from the sacral midline).
  • Congenital scoliosis with hemivertebra progressing >10 degrees/year or curve >40 degrees at the end of growth.
  • Neuromuscular scoliosis (e.g., cerebral palsy) with curve >40-50 degrees limiting sitting or respiratory function.
  • Surgical objective: instrumented arthrodesis with fusion of the greater curve and preservation of flexible compensatory curves.

items.title: Marfan syndrome and scoliosis
items.contentHtml:
Marfan syndrome and scoliosis

  • Marfan syndrome is a connective tissue disease caused by a mutation in fibrillin-1 (FBN1 gene); Scoliosis is a major musculoskeletal manifestation.
  • Prevalence of scoliosis in Marfan: 40-60%; Curves are usually double (thoracic and lumbar) and progress rapidly.
  • Earlier surgical indications: Cobb angle >40-45 degrees due to risk of progression and cardiovascular complications (aortic dilation).
  • Cheating examination: in Marfan, the orthopedic corset is less effective due to ligamentous laxity; surgery is common.
  • Mandatory presurgical evaluation: echocardiogram to rule out aortic aneurysm; Avoid surgery if aortic diameter >45-50 mm.

items.title: Scheuermann's disease (juvenile kyphosis)
items.contentHtml:
Scheuermann's disease (juvenile kyphosis)

  • Scheuermann's disease (juvenile kyphosis): osteochondrosis of the vertebral bodies causing rigid kyphosis in adolescents.
  • Radiographic criteria: anterior wedging of at least 3 consecutive vertebral bodies >5 degrees, Schmorl's nodes (intravertebral disc herniations) and plateau irregularity.
  • Clinic: thoracic kyphosis >40-45 degrees (Cobb measurement in profile), pain in the thoracic region that worsens with extension.
  • Differential diagnosis: postural kyphosis (flexible, without wedging or Schmorl's nodes).
  • Treatment: physiotherapy for curves <50 degrees; Milwaukee brace if >50-60 degrees and immature skeleton; surgery (osteotomy + fusion) if >70 degrees or intractable pain.

items.title: Spondylolisthesis and spondylolysis
items.contentHtml:
Spondylolisthesis and spondylolysis

  • Spondylolysis: defect in the pars interarticularis of the vertebral arch (generally L5), often due to repetitive stress in lumbar extension (gymnasts, lifters).
  • Spondylolisthesis: anterior sliding of one vertebra on the lower one, secondary to bilateral spondylolysis (isthmic) or other causes (degenerative, dysplastic).
  • Meyerding classification: grade I (0-25% slip), II (26-50%), III (51-75%), IV (76-100%) and V (spondyloptosis).
  • Clinical: low back pain that worsens with extension, sciatica if there is root compression.
  • Treatment: conservative (rest, physiotherapy) for grades I-II; Surgery (pars fusion or repair) if persistent pain or progressive slipping >50%.

items.title: Congenital kyphosis and segmentation defects
items.contentHtml:
Congenital kyphosis and segmentation defects

  • Congenital kyphosis: deformity due to vertebral anomalies present at birth; It may be due to formation defects (posterior hemivertebra) or segmentation defects (anterior or posterolateral bar).
  • segmentation defects (failure to separate vertebrae) produce a bone bar that limits anterior growth, generating progressive kyphosis.
  • Congenital kyphosis has a high risk of progression and spinal cord compression. (myelopathy) especially in curves >50 degrees.
  • Diagnosis: x-ray and CT scan to evaluate bar; MRI to rule out intraspinal abnormalities (diastematomyelia).
  • Treatment: early surgery (hemivertebrectomy or rod resection + fusion) before the age of 5 years to avoid severe progression.

items.title: Lenke classification for idiopathic scoliosis
items.contentHtml:
Lenke classification for idiopathic scoliosis

  • Lenke classification: system that integrates the type of curve (1-6), lumbar modifier (A, B, C) and sagittal thoracic (-, N, +).
  • Type 1: major thoracic curve (structure); Type 2: double thoracic (proximal and main thoracic, both structural); Type 3: double major (structural thoracic and lumbar); Type 4: major triple; Type 5: thoracolumbar/higher lumbar; Type 6: thoracolumbar/higher lumbar with structural thoracic.
  • Lumbar modifier A: C7 plumb falls between pedicles; B: touches the medial edge of the pedicle; C: outside the vertebral body.
  • Thoracic sagittal modifier: kyphosis T5-T12 <10 degrees (-), 10-40 degrees (N), >40 degrees (+).
  • Usefulness: guides the selection of fusion levels; For example, in type 1 with lumbar A and sagittal N modifier, only the thoracic curve is fused.

items.title: Orthopedic Treatment Options: Milwaukee Brace
items.contentHtml:
Orthopedic treatment options (Milwaukee brace)

  • Milwaukee brace: cervicothoracolumbosacral orthosis (CTLSO) with cervical ring, anterior and posterior bar, and pressure pads.
  • Indicated in high thoracic curves (apex at T7 or higher) and in skeletally immature patients (Risser 0-2) with Cobb angle between 20-40 degrees.
  • Objective: stop the progression of the curve; does not correct the established deformity.
  • Efficacy: reduces the risk of progression to surgery in adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (BRAIST study).
  • Alternatives: Boston corset (thoracolumbosacral, more used for thoracolumbar and lumbar curves); Charleston corset (night, for flexible curves).
  • Examination trap: the Milwaukee corset requires use 18-23 hours/day; Early abandonment is the main cause of failure.
